speaker: interviewer
text: Tape seven, the survivor is Samuel Friedman. Why did you give your testimony today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think only because I want to leave, to the posterity what's happening-- what happened and how it was, and to be a live witness so that nobody will come, later on, and tell, well it didn't happen at all. This is ridiculous. We knew what was happening. We didn't know the whole story. But we realized after the war, very, very quickly, that our families were destroyed and murdered by the Germans. And don't say Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you elaborate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because people are always saying, the Nazis have done this, and the Nazis have done that. And later, the generation will ask, where are the Nazis from? From Mars or from-- they were not-- not-- not Nazis. Nazi's only a person who belonged to the NSDAP party. But the Germans did it, and willingly, and they were happy. I saw soldiers pushing and shoving and beating people. Soldiers, not the SS, not the SA. Not only.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has the Holocaust affected your faith, your religious beliefs?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have religious belief. I don't have religious belief.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if I know stories about children taken away from mothers, and the mothers and the fathers have to survive this and to carry on, later on, like my own [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cousin, well, where was the god? How can you believe in God or something? This is ridiculous. I don't even discuss it. Don't even want to discuss it. People who believe in God, I would leave them believe as much as you want. I wouldn't interfere with anybody. Somebody wants to believe in him, well, that's very nice of you. Be happy. You see, this is the difference between maybe racist-- racist and the people who are not racist. I don't care about the Chinese being Buddhist or something if they like it.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Jewish tradition, for example, I like very much the tradition. Why? Because we were brought up in this tradition. We know what is Hanukkahs and [INAUDIBLE] and things like this is. And we like to continue. I celebrate in Hanukkah and bring a few dollars to the children as the Hanukkah gelt, as we called it. Or we liked to do the Seder Pesach, which I think this is a historical tradition. Because eventually the tribes of Israel left Egypt, some time ago, in these conditions or these conditions. And the miracles, well, it's ridiculous. What about Friday night, today? In your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today is less than before. When the children were at home, this was a different story. But today, I am not doing this because we are on our own. We are going out, and many times we are going to the children to celebrate. But the children do celebrate Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a message for people that are watching your testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the message is how I survived is only by daring. I think the problem was dare or not to dare. If you dare, you have a chance. If you don't dare, you-- you are-- if you don't put your money on the horses, how can you win? But if you put your money on the horses, you may win and you may lose. So I decided not to give up, and to go and to-- to try to escape. And I must tell you, that at this time, I wasn't very cocky about this. I was very sad. And I decided well, even in my room in Germany, every night I switched off the-- the globe and left the window slightly open, in case somebody comes and wants to catch me or see me, or so I am through the window, running away. This was my policy. I run away, they will shoot me. Good luck on you, but they want to keep me in camps and torture me. Another thing I must add to this one, is that my looks, as non-Jewish, this was a very, very big contribution to this because some people couldn't get out. This poor bitter man, who wrote the-- the book about himself and his experience in Ghetto Lodz, of course he couldn't go out. He would be caught in five minutes. So the combination of I was looking good, if you call it, and daring. This is-- The message is try as much as you can in life or in business. Try and don't give up. You may win and you may lose but at least you tried. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what? Can you-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of this block where my father's business was and where we lived. On the right-hand side, you can see a pillar at the shop. On the left-hand side, you see houses. On the third floor, we used to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob Friedman, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the town again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kalisz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kalisz, Poland. And this is the place of 11 November.

speaker: interviewer
text: When is this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know when because this is from the book. But this is the picture I remember, and this is the picture as it exists there today. Nothing has changed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of my parents, my sister, on the left, and my girlfriend, between my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the names of the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The names are Jacob and Rose Friedman, Eugenia Friedman, Franca [? Valjowicz. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Franca?

speaker: interviewee
text: She perished together with the rest of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: When is this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Ghetto Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the picture survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was sent to me, by mail, to Kremenets, in '41. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Myself, in '27, I think, on vacation, on the farm of my uncle, who had the [INAUDIBLE] horse. We used to go there many times, and we spend a wonderful time there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself. The picture was taken in November, '42, in Friedland, in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in-- in '43, probably, in Friedland in Germany, in Winter Garden, where we used to assemble, and to listen to music and meet friends. This [? Mietek, ?] who is between the girls, he told me that he knows that I am Jewish, that it doesn't matter to him. He did not survive the war. He was arrested by the German Gestapo, put into concentration camp. We send him parcels, but he didn't come back. His fiance, Nina, on right, came to us and told us the story. The other Russian girl, the other girl on left is [? Shira. ?] She married the chap above her. He was a very good friend of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I am? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Aha, the chap on left with the pipe. On top.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself and my wife, Henrika, on the wedding day.

speaker: interviewer
text: When and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: 15 of July, '45, in Krakow, Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is myself in 1949? Police academy in Shefa-Amr. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Myself in Jerusalem, '63. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My eldest daughter, Eugenia, her husband, Graham, son, on the left, Andrew. On the right, Gavin and Michelle, daughter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The pictures were taken on the  same day or the same week last year, to celebrate our 50th wedding anniversary, as a gift to us. Here, on this picture, we can see my son, Jacob, and his wife, Rose. Sons-- on the left, David. On the right, Michael. This picture was taken in 1995, as a gift to us on our 50th wedding anniversary. On this picture, we can see my daughter, Ruth, her husband, Peter, and sons, the small, Max, and the-- the larger-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] blockage-- James.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Friedman, thank you very much for your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much for your trouble.
